email.subject: Daily Journey: Walker Miles on www.skechers.com

review.raw_markdown:
--- # Walker Miles — Skechers.com Site Journey Review **Date: April 5, 2026 | Yesterday's Score: 4/10** --- ## 1. Executive Summary Another rough day on skechers.com. The front half of the trip — homepage, navigation, product page — held up reasonably well. Slip-ins are right there front and center on the homepage, which I appreciate. But then I tried to check my cart and got a completely blank white screen. Nothing. Just... gone. That's the same core breakdown I hit yesterday. And when I searched "slip in," the results came back showing women's shoes first — even though I'm logged in as a Skechers Plus member. This site still doesn't know who I am once I hit checkout or search. --- ## 2. Business Impact Score: 4/10 **No change from yesterday.** The cart failure alone kills this score. A customer who can't see their cart can't buy anything. That's a conversion dead end. --- ## 3. What's Working - **Slip-ins front and center on the homepage.** The hero section shows "HANDS FREE Slip-ins" in big, bold letters right below the fold. I saw it immediately. That's exactly what I'm looking for. Good start. - **BOGO 50% OFF deal is persistent and visible.** That blue banner stays at the top across every page. As someone who pays attention to value, I noticed it every step of the way. That's effective promotion. - **Men's navigation menu is clean and readable.** The hamburger menu opens to a simple, well-spaced list. "Shoes," "Comfort Technologies," "Hands Free Slip-ins" — all there, all legible without squinting. Touch targets feel comfortable. This is one of the better-designed parts of the mobile experience. - **Product page (Arch Fit 2.0 - Azusah) is solid.** Large product name in readable font, price ($135) displayed clearly, the BOGO discount called out directly on the page, and a big product image. The "Hands Free Slip-ins" badge is a nice trust signal. 4 stars and 10 reviews visible immediately — that helps me feel confident. - **Size grid is functional.** Good-sized tap targets for each half-size from 7.0 to 14.0. I could tap these without fumbling. The "Please select a size" error message appeared clearly in red — the system caught that I hadn't picked a size yet. - **ADD TO CART button is large and unmissable.** Full-width, bold blue — exactly how it should be on mobile. - **263 results for Men's Hands Free Slip-ins.** Good selection depth. Knowing there are over 250 options gives me confidence I'll find my fit. --- ## 4. What's Weak - **The cart page is completely blank.** Step 8 showed a totally white screen — no products, no totals, no checkout button, nothing. This is a critical failure. I added a shoe to my cart and then had nowhere to go. This is the same problem as yesterday and it has not been fixed. - **Search shows women's shoes first.** I searched "slip in" and the top results were both labeled "Women's." I'm a logged-in male Skechers Plus member. The site knows this. There's no excuse for surfacing women's styles at the top of my results. It returned 1,037 results — great selection — but the ordering completely ignores my gender. - **Login didn't change anything visible.** The Step 3 login screenshot looks identical to the homepage. There's no visible "Welcome back, Walker" moment, no personalized banner, no Skechers Plus member acknowledgment. After logging in, the site looks exactly like it does for a stranger. - **"Dismiss Popups" step didn't show a real popup.** The screenshot between homepage and logged-in state just shows a banner change (from "BOGO 50% OFF" to "Free Shipping with Skechers Plus"). There may have been a popup that didn't fully render, or the experience was underwhelming — but I never got a moment that said "hey, we see you're a Plus member." - **"Spring Style Guide" promotion is irrelevant to me.** That's the secondary banner message I kept seeing. That's for a different customer. I want comfort tech, slip-ins, wide fit. Spring style guides feel aimed at fashion shoppers, not someone like me who just wants shoes that don't aggravate my feet. - **Medium width filter gets cut off on the Men's Shoes page.** The width filter buttons show "Wide Fit," "Extra Wide Fit," and then "Medium" is clipped at the right edge of the screen. A customer who needs medium width might not even see that option without scrolling horizontally — which nobody expects to do. --- ## 5. Recommendations 1. **Fix the cart page immediately.** This is a revenue blocker. Every customer who adds an item and goes to checkout is hitting a blank wall. Priority one, full stop. 2. **Respect gender in search results.** A logged-in male user searching "slip in" should see men's results first. Basic personalization. 3. **Create a logged-in "welcome back" moment.** Show my name, my Plus status, maybe my points balance. Make me feel like a member, not a stranger. 4. **Fix the width filter cutoff on the Men's Slip-ins category page.** "Medium" width is being clipped — add horizontal scroll indicator or wrap the filter buttons. 5. **Swap the "Spring Style Guide" banner for something relevant to a returning Plus member.** BOGO is already doing the deal work — use the secondary banner for loyalty messaging or comfort tech highlights. --- ## 6. Bottom Line The site knows how to show me what I want at the top of the funnel — slip-ins, BOGO deals, clean men's navigation. But it completely falls apart when it matters most: the cart is broken, search ignores who I am, and logging in gives me nothing. I'm a loyal Skechers Plus member and the site treats me like a first-time visitor the moment things get serious. **Score: 4/10 — No change from yesterday.** The cart failure is unchanged. Until that's fixed, this journey cannot score higher than a 5 regardless of everything else. One step forward on the homepage, same brick wall at checkout. --- ## 7. Evidence **Step 1 — Homepage** Opened to the Skechers homepage. "BOGO 50% OFF Applied at Cart" banner across the top. The hero section showed a man and woman in casual clothes, and below that a large "HANDS FREE Slip-ins" heading with the Arch Fit logo starting to show. Also a "Spring Style Guide! Shop Now" secondary banner. Logo, search icon, account icon, cart (showing 0 items), and hamburger menu in the nav bar. Clean and uncluttered. Slip-ins were front and center — that's exactly right for me. Text was large enough to read. Touch targets in the nav looked adequate. **Step 2 — Dismiss Popups** The page looked nearly identical to Step 1, except the secondary banner changed from "Spring Style Guide!" to "Free Shipping with Skechers Plus Join Now for free." No visible popup overlay was dismissed — whatever appeared may have auto-closed or wasn't captured. The main hero image and Slip-ins section unchanged. Not a meaningful step from what I could see. **Step 3 — Log In** The screenshot after logging in looks identical to the homepage. No confirmation of who I am, no "Welcome back, Walker," no Plus member status displayed. The cart icon still shows 0. There's no indication the site recognizes me as a returning loyalty customer. This is a missed opportunity every single time. **Step 4 — Men's Category** The hamburger menu opened to a clean, vertical list: MEN at the top, then Shoes, Shop by Activity, Comfort Technologies, Collections, Collaborations, Clothing & Accessories — each with a right-arrow chevron. At the bottom, without arrows: Hands Free Slip-ins, Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit as quick links. Very easy to read. Well spaced. I could tap "Shoes" or go straight to "Hands Free Slip-ins" without any confusion. This menu is genuinely well done. **Step 5 — Men's Hands Free Slip-ins Category Page** Page header said "Hands Free Slip-ins" with a good description of the technology. Three width filter buttons visible: Wide Fit, Extra Wide Fit, and "Medium" was cut off at the right edge. 263 results shown with a toggle for "Free pickup at Set Location." Filter and Sort by controls present. Two shoe thumbnails just starting to appear at the bottom of the screen. The BOGO banner still present at top. Easy to read, though the clipped width filter is a problem. **Step 6 — Product Page (Arch Fit 2.0 - Azusah)** Clear product name in large text. $135.00 price prominent. 4-star rating with 10 reviews, and "Add to Wishlist" link. "Buy 1, Get 1 50% OFF! Applied at Cart" shown in red — directly reinforcing the banner deal. Large product photo of a white/gray shoe. "Hands Free Slip-ins" brand badge visible. This page gave me everything I needed to evaluate the product. Well organized for mobile. **Step 7 — Add to Cart** The size selection grid was shown. Sizes from 7.0 through 14.0 in a clean grid layout — boxes are a good size for tapping. A red/orange warning icon with "Please select a size" appeared, indicating I hadn't picked one yet. QTY set to 1. Shipping and in-store pickup options shown. The "ADD TO CART" button filled the bottom of the screen in bold blue — very easy to tap. The size selection experience was straightforward, though the error message did appear, meaning the automation tried to add before selecting. **Step 8 — Cart** Completely blank white screen. No header, no nav bar, no cart items, no totals, no checkout button. Nothing loaded. This is a total failure. A customer at this stage is ready to buy and hits an empty page. This exact problem was present yesterday and remains unfixed today. **Step 9 — Search ("slip in")** Search returned 1,037 results for "SLIP IN" — that's a solid number. But the first two visible products were both labeled "Women's": "Skechers Slip-ins: Arch Fit Arcade - See Ya There" and "Skechers Slip-ins: Contour Foam - Cozy Fit." Both also labeled "Also in Wide." As a logged-in male customer, I should not be seeing women's shoes at the top of my search results. The search volume is there, but relevance ranking is completely ignoring my account gender. Filter and Sort controls are available, but I shouldn't have to use them to get men's results. --- ## Technical Audit ## Technical Summary Platform: Salesforce Commerce Cloud (Demandware). Journey covers 8 steps with generally low TTFB (28–47ms), except the Cart page (140ms). The audit surfaced 32 axe violations, 6 unique 429 errors (bot detection and cart endpoint), and a CORS misconfiguration on a third-party widget. LCP and CLS were not captured, leaving Core Web Vitals incomplete. --- ## Accessibility **Critical (4 violations):** - `aria-required-parent` (Homepage): ARIA roles used outside their required parent container — likely a slider/carousel component with misstructured list roles. - `aria-required-children` (View Product): Element with role that requires specific child roles contains none (e.g., `role="listbox"` without `role="option"` children). - `aria-valid-attr-value` (View Product): One or more ARIA attributes set to invalid values — needs targeted DOM inspection to identify which attributes. - `button-name` (View Product): Buttons with no accessible name (no text, `aria-label`, or `aria-labelledby`). Common in icon-only UI elements like close/expand buttons. **Serious (5 violations):** - `link-name` (Homepage): Anchor elements with no discernible text — likely image links with no `alt` text on the child ``. - `link-in-text-block` (Men's Shoes): In-text links distinguished only by color, not underline or other non-color indicator. Fails WCAG 1.4.1. - `aria-dialog-name` (View Product): Modal/dialog missing `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`. Screen readers cannot announce dialog purpose. - `aria-input-field-name` (View Product): ARIA input fields (e.g., `role="spinbutton"` for quantity) without accessible name. - `color-contrast` (View Product): Text failing minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio (WCAG AA). **Moderate (11+ violations):** - Landmark structure broken across Homepage and Men's Shoes: duplicate `
`, nested `
` not top-level, non-unique landmarks, content outside landmark regions. Indicates components rendering their own `
` or `
` in isolation without considering page-level structure. - `heading-order` (Homepage): Heading levels skip (e.g., `

` → `

`), likely from reusable components that assume a different heading hierarchy. --- ## SEO - `lang="en"` present on `` — correct. - `heading-order` violations affect crawler content hierarchy parsing. - LCP not captured — cannot assess Largest Contentful Paint, a direct Core Web Vitals ranking signal. - No canonical, robots, or structured data visible in truncated source; cannot audit these without full ``. - Demandware URL structure is standard; no anomalies visible. --- ## Performance | Step | TTFB | |---|---| | Homepage | 44ms | | Log In | 31ms | | Men's Category | 31ms | | Men's Shoes | 47ms | | View Product | 28ms | | Add to Cart | 28ms | | **View Cart** | **140ms** | | Search | 38ms | - **Cart TTFB is 3–5× higher than all other pages.** At 140ms this indicates server-side cart computation (session resolution, inventory check, pricing) before first byte. Not critical but worth optimizing given cart abandonment sensitivity. - **LCP and CLS not recorded for any step** — the most impactful Core Web Vitals signals are entirely absent from this audit data. - **Third-party script count is high:** GTM, GA4, DC Floodlight, Google Ads, TikTok Pixel (3 scripts), Pinterest, Reddit Pixel, Bing UET, Yahoo YTC, Impact, Quantum Metric, Monetate (2 scripts), Bloomreach, Snapchat, Facebook Events, Nextdoor, Taboola, Stape, Tune, WebFont Loader — 20+ async third-party scripts on category pages. These don't block parse but do compete for bandwidth and CPU on mobile. - **Yahoo YTC script loaded twice** on Homepage and Log In (`s.yimg.com/wi/ytc.js` appears as two separate tags). Duplicate pixel firing. - **`tags.creativecdn.com` loaded twice** on Men's Category page. Duplicate tag — likely a GTM configuration error. - **Monetate loads two scripts** (`entry.js` + `custom.js`) — may be intentional but worth confirming no duplication. - **5 weights of Mulish** loaded via WebFont Loader on every page. Subset or reduce if not all weights are used. --- ## Mobile Optimization - `--vh: 6.640000000000001px` set as inline CSS on `` — this is a JavaScript-calculated viewport height workaround for iOS Safari's dynamic address bar. The floating-point precision (`6.640000000000001`) is a minor issue; `6.64px` would be equivalent and cleaner, but functionally inconsequential. - No `` visible in truncated source; assumed present but not confirmed. - No mobile-specific structural issues evident from HTML samples beyond the accessibility violations above, which compound on mobile due to AT usage patterns. --- ## Console & Network Errors **429 Rate Limiting — Kasada Bot Detection (5 errors):** - Endpoint: `/149e9513-01fa-4fb0-aad4-566afd725d1b/2d206a39-8ed7-437e-a3be-862e0f06eea3/fp?x-kpsdk-v=j-1.2.308` - This is Kasada's client-side fingerprinting SDK (`kpsdk`). The 429s indicate the bot detection layer flagged the audit session and rate-limited its fingerprinting calls. These errors will occur in any automated/headless context. However, if real user sessions are triggering these (e.g., fast navigation, VPN users), it could degrade client-side bot token acquisition and interfere with protected endpoints. **429 Rate Limiting — Cart Page (1 error):** - Endpoint: `https://www.skechers.com/cart/` - The cart page itself returned 429. Combined with the elevated 140ms TTFB, this suggests the cart endpoint has aggressive rate limiting that may affect legitimate users under load or with fast repeated navigation. Needs verification against actual rate limit thresholds. **CORS Violation — Stylitics Widget (2 errors + 2 net::ERR_FAILED):** ``` Access to CSS stylesheet at 'https://web-assets.stylitics.com/style.css?base=hotspots&version=320dd7a42c' has been blocked by CORS policy: No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header ``` - Stylitics is a third-party shoppable styling widget. Their CDN is not returning `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` for `skechers.com`. The widget's CSS fails to load, causing the widget to render without styles (or not render at all). This is a vendor-side configuration issue — Stylitics needs to add `skechers.com` to their CORS allowlist, or the request mode needs to change from CORS to `no-cors` (though that prevents reading the response). File a support ticket with Stylitics. --- ## Recommendations **Priority 1 — Critical accessibility (WCAG blocker):** 1. Fix `button-name` on View Product: add `aria-label` to all icon-only buttons. 2. Fix `aria-required-children` on View Product: ensure `role="listbox"` / `role="tablist"` wrappers contain correctly-roled children. 3. Fix `aria-valid-attr-value` on View Product: audit all ARIA attributes in the product page component for invalid values. 4. Fix `aria-required-parent` on Homepage: likely a carousel — wrap `role="option"` or `role="tab"` elements in their required parent roles. **Priority 2 — Functional errors:** 5. Contact Stylitics to resolve CORS on `web-assets.stylitics.com` — the widget is silently broken. 6. Investigate cart 429 threshold: determine whether the rate limit on `/cart/` is triggering for real users or only automated sessions. If the former, raise the threshold or move bot detection upstream. **Priority 3 — Data gaps:** 7. Capture LCP and CLS for all 8 steps. Without these, Core Web Vitals compliance cannot be assessed. Use Chrome DevTools, Lighthouse CI, or a real-user monitoring tool. **Priority 4 — Tag hygiene:** 8. Remove duplicate Yahoo YTC tag (fires twice on Homepage/Log In). 9. Remove duplicate `tags.creativecdn.com` tag on Men's Category (GTM audit needed). 10. Audit Monetate dual-script setup to confirm no duplicate event firing. **Priority 5 — Serious accessibility:** 11. Fix `link-name` on Homepage: add `alt` text to all linked images. 12. Fix `link-in-text-block` on Men's Shoes: add underline or other non-color differentiation to inline links. 13. Fix `aria-dialog-name` on View Product modals.
